ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cracker class; no specific sugar cracker subclass exists. FOODON_03301710 (cracker) is the closest defensible match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers the biscuits and crackers category. No sugar-cracker-specific code exists; this is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for sugar cracker identified; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sugar crackers is approximately 30 g, equivalent to 4–5 individual crackers, providing around 141 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Sugar Cracker — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database (cr_id F11080064)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record F11080064 for Sugar Cracker. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Crackers and Biscuits — University of Sydney GI Database; closest analogous entry: plain crackers / water crackers GI ~65–72
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for 'sugar cracker' exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous entries for plain wheat-flour crackers and sweet biscuits (GI range 55–72) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as (65 × 21.2 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 13.8. The input record's embedded GI value of 65 (confidence 0.577) is consistent with this analogous estimate and has been retained.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (588 mg/100 g) combined with high refined sugar (15.69 g/100 g) and zero dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid sugar crackers; sodium at 588 mg/100 g approaches the 600 mg clinical threshold, and the high refined-sugar load with absent fibre promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention, compounding blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (70.69 g/100 g), high sugar (15.69 g/100 g), zero dietary fibre, and medium-high GI of 65
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sugar crackers are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the combination of 70.69 g/100 g refined carbohydrate, 15.69 g/100 g sugar, zero fibre, and a GI of 65 produces rapid and significant postprandial glucose spikes with no fibre buffer to moderate absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g from shortening), saturated fat (~7 g/100 g from palm oil), high sodium (588 mg/100 g), and high refined sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sugar crackers pose a high cardiovascular risk due to the presence of trans fat from vegetable shortening, elevated saturated fat from palm oil, high sodium approaching clinical thresholds, and a high refined-sugar load — all of which independently and synergistically elevate LDL cholesterol, blood pressure, and systemic inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g), saturated fat (~7 g/100 g), and high refined sugar (15.69 g/100 g) are all pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sugar crackers are contraindicated for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; trans fat from shortening is strongly pro-inflammatory at any dose, palm-oil-derived saturated fat promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity, and refined sugar at 15.69 g/100 g further amplifies inflammatory mediators.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sugar Cracker is a sweet, crispy baked snack biscuit with a sugar coating, classified under biscuits, cookies and crackers. It is produced by baking a dough of refined wheat flour, sugar, vegetable shortening (typically palm-oil-based), salt, and leavening agents, then applying a sugar glaze or coating to the surface. The result is a light, crunchy, shelf-stable cracker with a distinctly sweet flavour and a slightly salty undertone. Per 100 g, sugar crackers provide 470 kcal, 7.84 g protein, 17.53 g fat (including approximately 7 g saturated fat and an estimated 0.3 g trans fat from shortening), 70.69 g carbohydrate, 15.69 g sugar, 0 g dietary fibre, and 588 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 13.8 per 30 g serving. Clinically, sugar crackers are a high-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The absence of dietary fibre means there is no buffer to moderate glucose absorption, and the high sodium content approaches clinical thresholds for blood pressure management. Trans fat from shortening is pro-inflammatory and contraindicated in inflammatory conditions. Sugar crackers are commonly consumed as a breakfast accompaniment or snack in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. They are vegetarian, vegan, and generally halal-suitable, but contain gluten and are not appropriate for gluten-free diets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a sugar cracker?
Is sugar cracker high in sodium?
Can I eat sugar crackers if I have high blood pressure?
Are sugar crackers suitable for rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080064) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database consistent with the embedded input value. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from typical palm-oil shortening composition for this cracker category, documented in notes.
notes: 1. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, micronutrient panels, and moisture data for this specific sugar cracker formulation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database record F11080064 or in USDA FoodData Central for a directly matching product. 2. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g is estimated at 7.0 g/100 g based on typical palm-oil-based shortening composition for wheat-flour crackers of this fat content (17.53 g total fat), consistent with USDA FoodData Central entries for comparable crackers (e.g., USDA SR Legacy item 18617, 'Crackers, standard snack-type'). 3. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g is estimated at 0.3 g/100 g based on typical partially hydrogenated vegetable shortening content in commercially produced sweet crackers; this is consistent with the input record's health suitability assessment. 4. CALCIUM AND IRON: calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record; these are confirmed zero values per the approved source. 5. DIETARY FIBRE: dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100 g as confirmed by the HPB source; this is a refined-flour product with no added fibre. 6. DIETARY TAG NOTE: The input record includes a 'wholegrain' dietary tag; this is inconsistent with the ingredient profile (refined wheat flour, zero fibre) and has been removed from the output. The 'high_sugar_warning' tag is retained as clinically appropriate. 7. GI ESTIMATION: GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (closest match: plain wheat crackers and sweet biscuits, GI 55–72); the input embedded GI of 65 at confidence 0.577 is consistent with this estimate. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (65 × 21.207 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.8. 8. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: Sugar cracker is a generic international snack product with no specific regional-language name required under the Veritas multilingual rules (not a Singaporean dish, Japanese dish, Korean dish, Indonesian dish, Thai dish, or Vietnamese dish). Local name fields are set to null accordingly.
